g.data-narration: The Byzantine Generals Problem is a famous computer science puzzle about how a group of leaders can agree on a plan despite some being traitors.
g.text: Introduced in 1982 by Lamport, Shostak, and Pease, this problem illustrates how distributed systems
can achieve consensus despite unreliable or malicious participants.
📍 Maps directly to: Blockchain networks, peer-to-peer systems, distributed databases

g.data-narration: Imagine Byzantine army generals surrounding an enemy city. They must decide: Attack or Retreat. Some generals might be traitors.
A traitor can send different messages to different generals, creating confusion. General D tells C 'Attack' but tells A 'Retreat'!
For any solution to work, two conditions must be met: all loyal generals agree on the same plan, and traitors cannot force a bad plan.
g.text: All loyal generals must decide on
If commander is loyal, every loyal

g.data-narration: The researchers proved consensus requires more than two-thirds honest participants. To tolerate m traitors, you need at least 3m+1 total nodes.
g.text: Example: For 1 traitor, you need 4 generals. For 2 traitors, you need 7 generals.

g.data-narration: This allegory maps perfectly to distributed computing: generals are nodes, messengers are network links, and traitors are faulty or malicious nodes.
Blockchain solves this by having nodes maintain a shared ledger. All honest nodes must agree on the same transaction history despite potential bad actors.
Bitcoin's Proof-of-Work makes attacking economically infeasible. Attackers need 51% of network computing power to overpower honest nodes.
g.text: Longest valid chain = Source of Truth (Greatest cumulative proof-of-work)

g.data-narration: This problem's solution enables all major cryptocurrencies to function securely. Its principles also apply to aviation, nuclear plants, and distributed databases.
In summary: the Byzantine Generals Problem is about achieving consensus with faulty nodes. Blockchain solves it through economic incentives and cryptographic proof.

g.text: Core Problem: Achieve consensus despite malicious participants
Math: Need >2/3 honest nodes (3m+1 to tolerate m traitors)
Blockchain maps: Generals → Nodes, Messages → Transactions
Bitcoin's PoW: Economic cost makes attacking infeasible for honest majority
"This is THE fundamental problem that makes decentralized digital money possible."
